comms_internal: This is a derived double-nominative construction in terms of Alpatov et al.’s grammar. In the online elicitation study, most respondents approved the binominative pattern. Alternatives are: i) “Watashi wa atama ga itam-u” [я TOP голова NOM болеть-PST] with the same NOM(NO.EXALT)_NOM pattern and ii) “Watashi wa zutsū ga su-ru [я TOP головная боль NOM VBZ-NPST], a translation that does not meet the inclusion criteria.
Alternative is the NOM_BARE construction: "Tarō wa infuruenza da." [PN TOP грипп COP.NPST]

This is a non-derived double-nominative construction in terms of Alpatov et al.’s grammar. In the online elicitation study, most respondents approved the binominative pattern. Alternatives include a constructions with the TR pattern, cf. a) “Tarō wa inu o kowa-gat-te-i-ru.” [Тароо TOP собака ACC страшный-VBZ.EVD-CVB-AUX.IMPFV-NPST] where the verb form is derived from kowai and b) “Tarō wa inu o osore-te-i-ru.” [Тароо TOP собака ACC бояться-CVB-IMPFV-NPST] where a different verb is used. The three constructions differs with respect to their evidentiality values, where the -gar morpheme signals the inferential meaning. AK: The construction with two nominative is more frequent than the TR constuction: 1,265 vs. 243.

AK: alternative is the TR pattern. However, the TR pattern is mainly used with more volitional situations of touching in case of concrete objects. NOM_DAT is more common with abstract nouns and in case of less volitional touching, as here. Apart from this, there are constructions with the verb “sawaru”, which implies more volitional situations of touching, similar to “grab, seize”. That said, “sawaru” can also be used in both the TR and NOM_DAT pattern.

comms_internal: Same verb as 17, different pattern. QUOT is materially identical with COM, and probably the two markers share the same origin. However, they differ in terms of their properties, e.g. QUOT can be easily combined with ACC. AK: “COM can also be combined with ACC (and in many cases indeed does) but it does not function as a substantivizer in that case, so the statement that they differ in their properties still holds.”

comms_internal: The DAT marker in this sentence can be omitted due to the usual “clash” between DAT and TOP. In Japanese, numeral constructions like “one dollar” are not marker by case postpositions. But an NP without a numeral would be marked with a NOM marker, cf. “Tarō (ni) wa o-kane ga tari-nai.” [Тароо (DAT) TOP HON-деньги NOM хватать-NEG]

comms_internal: This is a non-derived double-nominative construction in terms of Alpatov et al.’s grammar. In the online elicitation study, most respondents approved the binominative pattern (and some preferred the TR alternative). Alternative is the verbal construction with the TR pattern: “Tarō wa Hanako o kirat-te-i-ru” [Тароо TOP Ханако ACC ненавидеть-CVB-AUX.IMPFV-NPST]. AK: Intuitively the non-verbal construction with the NOM_NOM(NO.EXALT) pattern is more common, cf. frequencies: ga kirai 916 (incl. 14 entries with HUMAN NAMES) vs. o kirau 855 (incl. 13 entries with HUMAN NAMES).

This corresponds to Shibatani’s (2001) characterization of this verb as the verb used in the binomnative pattern. In the online elicitation study, 7 out of 8 respondents chose the binominative pattern. None of the other alternative was commonly accepted.
